coredata.dc:title: Ni-Fe bimetallic hydrogel with bifunctional enzyme activity for colorimetric detection of phenolic pollutants
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiao-Juan
Long, Yan
Wei, Chuan-Wan
Gao, Shu-Qin
Lin, Ying-Wu
coredata.dc:description: Phenol-containing wastewater is an important source of water pollution in the environment. It is important and challenging to develop simple and rapid analytical methods for the detection of these trace contaminantsinnaturalenvironments. Natural enzymes are often selected for colorimetric detection due to their excellent catalytic activity along with corresponding color changes with special substrates. Compared with natural enzymes, artificial nanozymes have been widely concerned owing to the characteristics such as high stability, tunable catalytic activity, easy preparation and large-scale production. Herein, a bimetallic hydrogel 3-QW-NiFe with peroxidase-like and oxidase-like activities was designed and prepared by a simple and green synthesis method. The catalytic mechanism was investigated by various spectra including UV–vis, fluorescence, and EPR spectra, which suggested that the peroxidase-like activity and the oxidase-like activity of 3-QW-NiFe metallohydrogel were realized by the hydroxyl radical and superoxide anion produced in the reaction process respectively. Based on the peroxidase-like activity of 3-QW-NiFe, a colorimetric sensor was constructed for the discrimination of m-bihydroxybenzene with good selectivity and low detection limit (0.033 μM) in a linear range (0.5–12 μM). Furthermore, the oxidase-like activity was used for the colorimetric detection of hydroquinone. The smartphone-assisted detection method was also constructed, which helps to realize fast and on-site visual detection without the need of any analytical instruments. In addition, the assay based on 3-QW-NiFe metallohydrogel can be successfully applied to detect phenolic pollutants in the water samples. With these advantages and the convenient operation, the proposed colorimetric assay for the detection of m-AP and HQ in this study can be applied for real-time environmental monitoring, opening new avenues for the detection of phenol contaminantsinnaturalenvironments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ni-Fe bimetallic hydrogel
Bifunctional enzyme activity
Colorimetric detection
Smartphone-assisted detection
Phenolic pollutants

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and structural charaterization of 3-QW-NiFe metallohydrogel
Investigation of POD-like activity and catalytic mechanism of 3-QW-NiFe
Colorimetric discrimination of aminophenol isomers and detection of m-Ap
Quantitative detection of HQ using the oxidase-like activity of 3-QW-NiFe
Determination of m-Ap and HQ in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Elemental mapping of C, N, O, Fe, and Ni of 3-QW-NiFe xerogel. (b) IR spectra. (c) XRD pattern of 3-QW powder and 3-QW-NiFe xerogel. Full XPS survey spectra of 3-QW powder and 3-QW-NiFe xerogel (d), and high resolution spectra of Ni 2p (e) and Fe 2p (f), respectively.

(a) The UV–vis spectra of TMB + H2O2 (1), H2O2 + 3-QW-NiFe (2), and TMB + H2O2 + 3-QW-NiFe (3). Inset: Photo of the corresponding (1), (2), (3) three cases. (b) Fluorescence spectra of terephthalic acid with different concentrations of 3-QW-NiFe. (c) The UV–vis spectra of ox-TMB without or with a •OH radical scavenger (isopropyl alcohol (IPA)). (d) EPR spectra of H2O2 + 3-QW-NiFe + DMPO. (e) The UV–vis spectra of TMB (4), and TMB + 3-QW-NiFe (5). Inset: Photo of the corresponding (4), (5) two cases. (f) The UV–vis spectra of TMB + 3-QW-NiFe with air or N2 atmosphere. (g) The UV–vis spectra of ox-TMB without or with an O2 • − radical scavenger (benzoquinone (BQ)). (h) EPR spectra of 3-QW-NiFe + DMPO.

The UV–vis spectra of ox-ABTS (a) without and with m-AP, o-AP, p-AP, respectively. (b) The UV–vis spectra of ox-ABTS by adding different concentrations of m-AP (0.5–12 μM). (inset: the color change). (c) The linear calibration plot for m-AP detection. (d) The UV–vis spectra of ox-TMB without and with RC, CC, HQ, respectively. (e) The UV–vis spectra of ox-TMB by adding different concentrations of HQ (0.1–10 μM); (inset: the color change). (f) The linear calibration plot for HQ detection.
(a) Process of smartphone-assisted analysis for m-Ap. (b) Standard colorimetric card of m-AP. (c) Linear relationship curve between R/G values and m-AP concentrations.
Schematic illustration of double enzyme-like activities of 3-QW-NiFe metallohydrogel and its application in colorimetric discrimination of HQ and m-AP.
HQ detection limit comparison of the reported works with the proposed method.

head.ce:title: Ni-Fe bimetallic hydrogel with bifunctional enzyme activity for colorimetric detection of phenolic pollutants

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Protein Structure and Function of Universities in Hunan Province, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Protein Structure and Function of Universities in Hunan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Lab of Protein Structure and Function of Universities in Hunan Province, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, China (Ying-Wu Lin).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Phenol-containing wastewater is an important source of water pollution in the environment. It is important and challenging to develop simple and rapid analytical methods for the detection of these trace contaminantsinnaturalenvironments. Natural enzymes are often selected for colorimetric detection due to their excellent catalytic activity along with corresponding color changes with special substrates. Compared with natural enzymes, artificial nanozymes have been widely concerned owing to the characteristics such as high stability, tunable catalytic activity, easy preparation and large-scale production. Herein, a bimetallic hydrogel 3-QW-NiFe with peroxidase-like and oxidase-like activities was designed and prepared by a simple and green synthesis method. The catalytic mechanism was investigated by various spectra including UV–vis, fluorescence, and EPR spectra, which suggested that the peroxidase-like activity and the oxidase-like activity of 3-QW-NiFe metallohydrogel were realized by the hydroxyl radical and superoxide anion produced in the reaction process respectively. Based on the peroxidase-like activity of 3-QW-NiFe, a colorimetric sensor was constructed for the discrimination of m-bihydroxybenzene with good selectivity and low detection limit (0.033 μM) in a linear range (0.5–12 μM). Furthermore, the oxidase-like activity was used for the colorimetric detection of hydroquinone. The smartphone-assisted detection method was also constructed, which helps to realize fast and on-site visual detection without the need of any analytical instruments. In addition, the assay based on 3-QW-NiFe metallohydrogel can be successfully applied to detect phenolic pollutants in the water samples. With these advantages and the convenient operation, the proposed colorimetric assay for the detection of m-AP and HQ in this study can be applied for real-time environmental monitoring, opening new avenues for the detection of phenol contaminantsinnaturalenvironments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Phenol-containing wastewater is one of the most harmful industrial wastewater in the world, and it is an important source of water pollution in the environment [1]. Many industrial fields such as gas, coking, oil refining, metallurgy, machinery manufacturing, glass, petrochemical, wood fiber, chemical organic synthesis industry, plastics, medicine, pesticides, as well as paint discharge wastewater containing phenol. Aminophenol and resorcinol are common and harmful among many phenolic pollutants. Aminophenol isomers have been broadly applied in different fields as raw materials, which existed universally in ecological environment [2]. Owing to the strong penetrability, aminophenol isomers can easily penetrate through cell membranes and humans skin, leading to mutagenesis and genotoxicity [2,3]. Hydroquinone (1,4-dihydroxybenzene, HQ) was also important raw material in the field of cosmetics, pesticides, and pigments, while it was highly toxic and difficult to degrade [4]. The release of HQ to ecological circumstance was difficult to completely avoid during application, and it will cause great harm to the environment and organisms. Therefore, it is important and challenging to develop simple and rapid analytical methods for the detection of these trace contaminantsinnaturalenvironments. So far, many analytical methods have been developed to detect them, such as including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), electrochemistry methods, and fluorescence [5–7]. However, these detection methods often involved some disadvantages such as time consuming, complicated procedures, or expensive instruments. In contrast, the colorimetric detection method with advantages of being simple, rapid and visual was widely applied in environmental analysis [8–11]. Natural enzymes are often selected for colorimetric detection due to their excellent catalytic activity along with corresponding color changes with special substrates [12]. However, natural enzymes have the disadvantages of demanding harsh temperature and acidity, instability, and high price, which induced their limited application [13].

Compared with natural enzymes, artificial nanozymes have been widely concerned owing to the characteristics such as high stability, tunable catalytic activity, easy preparation and large-scale production [14–17]. Various nanomaterials such as nanoparticles, nanotubes, nanosheets have been found to possess enzymatic activity and used as functional nanozymes [18–24]. However, these materials often tended to aggregate, resulting in reduced catalytic activity [25]. Therefore, how to improve the enzyme-like activities and find a suitable dispersion medium to improve the dispersion of nanomaterials is very important. Usually, organics, polymers, and metal–organic frameworks were used as dispersion media [26–28]. Hydrogels contain fiber networks with a lot of porous structures and water molecules. It is because of the special network structure, hydrogels can be also used as dispersion media so as to avoid the aggregation of the prepared materials [29,30]. Metallohydrogels are composed of metallic ions and organic ligands through metal–ligand interactions and various non-covalent interactions. In metallohydrogels, metallic elements can be evenly dispersed in the fiber network, while giving the gel different functions including electric and magnetic properties, optical and redox properties, as well as catalytic activity [31–34]. The studies on the materials containing bimetallic center have drawn widespread attention due to the tunable properties [35–38]. Compared with single metals, the reactivity of polymetallic components can be improved through metal–metal synergistic interaction [39–41]. Therefore, it is significant to design and prepare a bimetallic hydrogel for colorimetric detection environmental pollutants.

Inspired by this, a bimetallic hydrogel (3-QW-NiFe) was prepared by simply mixing the gelator of a tryptophan derivative (3-QW) and metal ions (Ni2+ and Fe3+) (Scheme S1). The obtained 3-QW-NiFe was found to possess double enzyme activity (peroxidase-like and oxidase-like activity). The 3-QW-NiFe metallohydrogel could catalyze the oxidation of ABTS into blue ox-ABTS in the presence of H2O2. Meanwhile, 3-QW-NiFe exhibited the oxidase-like activity to convert colorless TMB into blue ox-TMB in the absence of H2O2. It was found that 3-QW-NiFe could be used for colorimetric discrimination of aminophenol isomers and quantitative detection of m-AP by its peroxidase-like activity. Hydroquinone (HQ) could also be quantitatively detected with low LOD (limit of detection) using the oxidase-like activity of 3-QW-NiFe. The detection of m-AP and HQ in actual samples further demonstrated the successful development of the colorimetric sensing platform based on the 3-QW-NiFe nanoenzyme. In addition, a smartphone-assisted assay method for the detection of m-AP and HQ was also constructed, which was simple and convenient and did not require large-scale instruments. The constructed colorimetric sensing platform was shown in Scheme 1 .

The materials and instruments involved in the study, the details about the synthesis of 3-QW and 3-QW-NiFe metallohydrogel, peroxidase-like and oxidase-like activity, catalytic mechanism of 3-QW-NiFe metallohydrogel, steady-state kinetic study, colorimetric detection and smartphone-assisted detection for m-AP and HQ, detection for phenolic contaminants in the actual samples are depicted in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and structural charaterization of 3-QW-NiFe metallohydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: 3-QW was obtained by referring to the previous procedure [42]. Mixing 3-QW (pH 9–10) and nickel chloride solution in a molar ratio of 2:1 gave a green metallic hydrogel (3-QW-Ni) with the help of sonication at room temperature (RT). Similarly, brown bimetallic hydrogel (3-QW-NiFe) was prepared by replacing nickel chloride with a mixture of nickel chloride and iron chloride under the same conditions. Nevertheless, 3-QW-Fe gel could not be obtained in the similar conditions. The xerogels of 3-QW-Ni and 3-QW-NiFe were obtained by simple freeze-drying method. SEM and TEM were used to study the microstructure of various materials. From Fig. S3, it was observed that 3-QW was composed of stacked blocks, and 3-QW-Ni as well as 3-QW-Fe presented interwoven short fiber owing to the presence of metal ions. Meanwhile, 3-QW-NiFe assembled to form networks with long fibers due to the synergistic effect of bimetallic Fe and Ni ions. From the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) elemental mapping of 3-QW-NiFe xerogel (Fig. 1 a), it can be seen that various elements, including C, N, O, Fe and Ni, were uniformly distributed in the gel network, indicating that hydrogels with network structure were excellent dispersion media. The surface area and pore size of 3-QW-NiFe were investigated via BET (Fig. S4). The BET surface area of 3-QW-NiFe was calculated to be 48.1834 m2/g and the average pore size was about 22.2979 nm.

As shown in Fig. 1b, the position and intensity of the peaks in the IR spectra changed when metal ions were introduced into 3-QW. The peak at 3376 cm−1 (–NH) decreased, indicating that H-bond interactions were weakened after addition of metal ions and the coordination interaction occurred between the amino N atoms and Fe3+ and Ni2+. The shift of the peak from 3171 cm−1 (–OH) to 3205 cm−1 and the decrease in the intensity of the characteristic peak of CO at 1719 cm−1 showed that the carboxyl oxygen atoms were involved in the coordination interaction. In addition, the stretching vibration of quinoline ring skeleton at 1582 cm−1 was shifted to 1589 cm−1, suggesting that the quinoline nitrogen atom is involved in the coordination.

XRD studies (Fig. 1c) showed that there are some sharp peaks representing microcrystalline structures in 3-QW, which was consistent with the microstructure from SEM. In contrast, a broad diffraction peak at about 20° presented in 3-QW-NiFe gel, indicating the amorphous nature of this metallohydrogel. Compared to 3-QW, the peak of d-spacing of 2.3 Å (representing the H-bonds interactions) decreased and a new peak at 32° presented in 3-QW-NiFe gel, which suggested the H-bonds interactions were weakened and metal–metal interactions were strengthened owing to the presence of metal ions.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out to determine the the chemical composition and valence state of 3-QW-NiFe metallohydrogel. From Fig. 1d, it was observed that the elements C, N, O, Fe and Ni are present in 3-QW-NiFe, which was consistent with the result of elemental mapping in Fig. 1a. The XPS spectra of Ni showed two fitted peaks corresponding to Ni 2p3/2 (856.0 eV) and Ni 2p1/2 (873.6 eV) and two satellite peaks, indicating that element Ni was present in the divalent form (Fig. 1e). There were two peaks with binding energy of 724.9 eV and 711.4 eV attributed to Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2. In addition, two satellite bands at 719.2 eV and 731.2 eV further indicated the identity of Fe3+ in the prepared 3-QW-NiFe metallohydrogel (Fig. 1f). The C 1 s spectra showed three fitted peaks at 284.5 eV, 285.6 eV and 288.1 eV, corresponding to C = N&C = C, C-N&C-C and C = O, (Fig. S5a). The N 1 s and O 1 s spectra were simulated with two peaks at (399.3 eV, 400.9 eV) and (531.4 eV, 533.2 eV), respectively (Fig. S5b–c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of POD-like activity and catalytic mechanism of 3-QW-NiFe
ce:section.ce:para: The POD-like activity of the 3-QW-NiFe bimetallic hydrogel was systematically evaluated by monitoring the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), the 3-QW-NiFe-TMB-H2O2 system exhibited the highest absorbance at 654 nm with the color change of the solution from colorless to blue. Nevertheless, 3-QW-NiFe-TMB and 3-QW-NiFe-H2O2 solutions remained colorless and the peak at 654 nm was almost negligible. These results showed that 3-QW-NiFe possessed POD-like activity. Meanwhile, the POD-like activity of 3-QW-NiFe was also demonstrated by the oxidation of ABTS. From Fig. S6, the color of ABTS became blue and distinct ox-ABTS characteristic peaks were at 415 nm and 727 nm. However, when monometallic gel 3-QW-Ni was added to the TMB-H2O2 (or ABTS-H2O2) system, the absorbance peak was weak and the solution color was baby blue (Fig. S7). As can be seen in Fig. S8, the zeta potentials of 3-QW-Ni and 3-QW-NiFe were 0.96 mV and −0.57 mV, respectively. The potential of 3-QW-NiFe was more negative than that of 3-QW-Ni, indicating that 3-QW-NiFe had good enzyme-like activity owing to the enhanced affinity for positively charged substances. As depicted in Fig. S9, the doping of normal transition metal ions (such as Cu2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Fe3+ and Ce3+) into 3-QW-Ni gel, most metal ions other than Fe3+ ions did not improve the catalytic activity of 3-QW-Ni gel. Nevertheless, the introduction of Fe3+ ions significantly increased the POD-like activity of 3-QW-Ni gel. The catalytic activity of 3-QW-NiFe bimetallic hydrogel when the ratio of Ni2+ and Fe3+ ions was 2:3 (Fig. S10).

Furthermore, the POD activity of 3-QW-NiFe hydrogel was affected by pH, temperature and TMB concentration. According to Fig. S11a, pH = 3.5 HAc/NaAc solution was selected for colorimetric detection. From Fig. S11b, it was observed that the catalytic activity of 3-QW-NiFe increased with increasing of temperature and reached the highest at 60 ℃. It is well known that natural enzymes are less active at low pH and high temperature, which severely limits their practical applications. In contrast, 3-QW-NiFe still possessed good catalytic properties under harsh conditions, which will broaden its application scope. For convenience, room temperature (25 ℃) was selected for the following experiment. In addition, the concentration of TMB also affected the catalytic activity (Fig. S11c). The absorbance at 654 nm increased as the concentration of TMB increased and reached a plateau when the concentration of TMB was 0.5 mM. Therefore, 0.5 mM was chosen as the optimal concentration for subsequent experiments. In addition, the optimal experimental conditions for the oxidation of ABTS catalyzed by 3-QW-NiFe at RT was explored. As shown in Fig. S12, HAc-NaAc (pH = 3.5) buffer solution, CABTS = 0.05 mM, CH2O2 = 1.0 mM and 10 min incubation time were chosen as subsequent experimental conditions.

Steady-state kinetic experiments were conducted to investigate the POD-like properties of 3-QW-NiFe metallohydrogel. The typical Michaelis-Menten curves (Fig. S13) were gained by varying the concentration of one substrate and fixing the concentration of the other substrates. The Michaelis constant (Km ) and the maximum velocity (Vmax ) were calculated by linear fitting of the Burke equation. Km is a crucial parameter of affinity, and lower Km values indicated that there was a strong affinity between 3-QW-NiFe and the substrates. In particular, the affinity of 3-QW-NiFe for the substrate H2O2 (Km = 0.242 mM) was more than 15 times that of HRP (Km = 3.702 mM). As listed in Table S1, the higher Vmax values of 3-QW-NiFe elucidated that 3-QW-NiFe metallohydrogel had high catalytic efficiency on the TMB-H2O2 system.

The catalytic mechanism of 3-QW-NiFe bimetallic hydrogels was investigated by UV–vis, fluorescence and EPR spectra. Then, terephthalic acid (TA) was collected as a fluorescence probe to monitor the production of •OH radical. As shown in Fig. 2b, the emission peak at 435 nm of hydroxyterephthalic acid was very weak in the absence of 3-QW-NiFe gel. In contrast, the peak gradually increased with increasing amount of 3-QW-NiFe gel. From Fig. 2c, it was observed that A654 decreased and the color changed from blue to yellow-green when •OH radical scavenger isopropyl alcohol (IPA) was added, suggesting that •OH radical was generated and the electron then was transferred to the substrate. 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolidine N-oxide (DMPO) was chosen as a trapping agent for the detection of •OH radicals. As can be seen from Fig. 2d, a typical quartet of characteristic peaks with relative signal intensities of 1:2:2:1 was observed in 3-QW-NiFe-H2O2 system. Nevertheless, the signal was silent in the absence of 3-QW-NiFe. These observations showed that •OH radicals played the role of electron transfer in the process of oxidation of TMB and 3-QW-NiFe was important to decompose and product •OH radicals.

The oxidase mimetic properties of 3-QW-NiFe were evaluated by direct oxidation of TMB in the absence of hydrogen peroxide. As shown in Fig. 2e, the absorbance at 654 nm of the 3-QW-NiFe system was much higher than that of the single TMB system, indicating that 3-QW-NiFe had oxidase-like activity. Subsequently, the oxidase-like activities of 3-QW-Ni, 3-QW-Fe, and 3-QW-NiFe with different the ratios of Ni and Fe were investigated under the same conditions. The absorbance of the bimetallic hydrogel 3-QW-NiFe + TMB system was significantly higher compared to the 3-QW-Ni + TMB and 3-QW-Fe + TMB systems, which might be due to the synergistic effect of two metal ions enhancing the catalytic property of the material. The experimental results showed that the catalytic efficiency of the obtained bimetallic gels by doping Ce3+, Mn2+, Co2+, and Cu2+ was not ideal, the oxidase-like activity of the 3-QW-NiFe bimetallic hydrogels was the best (Figs. S14a). The oxidase-like activity of 3-QW-NiFe was best when the molar ratio of Ni and Fe was 2:3 (Figs. S14b). The effect of pH, temperature and TMB concentration on the oxidase-like activity of 3-QW-NiFe was then investigated. The catalytic activity increased as increasing pH from pH 2.0 to pH 3.5 and decreased significantly when the pH was above 3.5 (Fig. S14c). The oxidase-like catalytic activity of 3-QW-NiFe was maintained at a steady state from 20 to 50 ℃ (Fig. S14d), indicating that the 3-QW-NiFe bimetallic hydrogel exhibited good catalytic activity over a wide temperature range. For experimental convenience, 25 ℃ was chosen for the next experiments. As can be seen from Fig. S14 e and f, the absorbance increased over the concentration range of 0.1–0.5 mM and reached a stable plateau when the concentration of TMB exceeded 0.5 mM. Based on the above analytical results, the following analytical experiments were carried out under optimal conditions (0.5 mM of TMB, pH = 3.5, 25 °C, 10 min).

Steady-state kinetic analysis was performed to further investigate the OXD-like activity of 3-QW-NiFe by varying the TMB concentration. The K m (0.069 mM) and V max (1.65 × 10-8 Ms−1) of 3-QW-NiFe were calculated by the linear Burke equation (Fig. S15), indicating that 3-QW-NiFe has high affinity and catalytic activity for TMB. The results were comparable or even better than other reported oxidase analogues (Table S2). Either as peroxidase or oxidase, 3-QW-NiFe xerogel remains more than 95 % active In addition, the catalytic activity of 3-QW-NiFe xerogel could still reach 95 % even after 35 days of storage (Figs. S16 and S17), showing good catalytic stability.

An oxygen dependent study was performed to investigate whether the oxidase mimetic activity of 3-QW-NiFe was dependent on oxygen. Fig. 2f gave the UV–vis absorbance spectra for the oxidation reaction of the 3-QW-NiFe + TMB system incubated for 10 min in air and nitrogen, respectively. It was observed that the oxidation process of TMB was inhibited in N2 saturated solution, which elucidated dissolved oxygen was very essential for enhancing the oxidase-like activity of 3-QW-NiFe. The reason for the importance of dissolved oxygen might be due to the transfer of electrons from the 3-QW-NiFe to dissolved oxygen, and then finishing the conversion of reactive oxygen species (ROS). Fig. 2g showed that the absorbance of 3-QW-NiFe-TMB decreased in the presence of HQ, indicating that superoxide radical anions (O2
• − ) was generated in the TMB oxidation process. Thus, 3-QW-NiFe might catalyze dissolved oxygen to O2
• − , which further oxidized TMB to blue ox-TMB. Besides, the nature of ROS was further identified by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolidine N-oxide (DMPO) as the trapping agent (Fig. 2h). The EPR signals of 3-QW-NiFe-TMB system generated by the combination of DMPO and O2
• − exhibited a typical quadruplet peak with a ratio of 1:1:1:1, which was larger than that without 3-QW-NiFe. Thus, as with most reported oxidase analogues, the oxidase-like activity of 3-QW-NiFe followed a free radical oxidation mechanism. Briefly, dissolved O2 first received an electron under the synergistic catalysis of Fe3+ and Ni2+ in 3-QW-NiFe metallohydrogel to form O2
• − , and then O2
• − immediately oxidized TMB to blue ox-TMB by a dehydrogenation process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric discrimination of aminophenol isomers and detection of m-Ap
ce:section.ce:para: The colorimetric discrimination study of aminophenol isomers was performed based on 3-QW-NiFe-ABTS-H2O2 system. Aminophenol isomers with the same concentration (20 μg/mL) were added to the ox-ABTS solution incubating for 5 min, respectively. It was observed that the solution color changed from blue to yellow, pink and colorless in the presence of o-AP, m-AP, and p-AP, which can be used to visually distinguish aminophenol isomers by naked-eye (the illustration in Fig. 3 ). As shown in Fig. 3a, the absorption peaks of ox-ABTS decreased significantly after the introduction of o-AP and p-AP. Nevertheless, in the presence of m-AP, the peaks at 415 nm and 727 nm decreased, and a new absorption band at 513 nm was produced. From above, the color change of ox-ABTS can be used to distinguish aminophenol isomers by naked-eye detection, while the UV–vis absorption spectra can be used for quantitative detection of aminophenol isomers. Although o-AP, m-AP and p-AP isomers all caused different decreases in A727, the addition of m-AP resulted in the appearance of a new UV–Vis peak at 513 nm with a distinct color change from blue to pink. Therefore, the colorimetric assay based on the 3-QW-NiFe-ABTS-H2O2 system was constructed for quantitative discrimination of m-AP.

Different concentrations of m-AP standard samples were added into the ox-ABTS solution, and the UV–vis absorbance spectra were recorded after 10 min of reaction. As shown in Fig. 3b, the A415 and A727 gradually decreased with increasing the concentration of m-AP, while the absorption of the new peak at 513 nm increased accompanied with a distinct color change from green to bluish violet to wine (inset in Fig. 3b). A linear relationship was established between lg(A513/A415) or lg(A513/A727) and C m
-Ap (0.5 μM-12.0 μM) and the linear regression equations for the colorimetric detection of the m-AP standard samples were shown in Fig. 3c. The linear regression equations for the colorimetric assay were lg(A513/A727) = 0.0752 C m
-AP − 0.3664 (R2 = 0.9987) and lg(A513/A415) = 0.0673 C m
-AP − 0.7674 (R2 = 0.9978). The limit of detection (LOD) for m-AP with 3-QW-NiF-ABTS-H2O2 systemwas 0.033 μM (S/N = 3), which was superior to the reported methods previously [2,43].

In addition to sensitivity, the selectivity and anti-interference properties were important, which was evaluated by studying the effect of some coexisting substances. The introduction of m-Ap resulted in a new absorption band at 513 nm that was far from the characteristic absorption of ox-ABTS. Therefore, 513 nm was chosen owing to higher sensitivity and lower interference. As shown in Fig. S18, the addition of different reducing substances to the ox-ABTS system, did not produce the absorption peak at 513 nm except for m-AP, proving that 3-QW-NiFe-ABTS-H2O2 system could exclusively be used to visually detect m-AP with specific selectivity. In addition, in the presence of these interfering substances, the absorbance at 513 nm was almost unaffected, even when the amount of the added interfering substances was 10 times that of m-AP. These results indicated that 3-QW-NiFe-ABTS-H2O2 system will be an excellent colorimetric sensor for the detection of m-AP with good selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of HQ using the oxidase-like activity of 3-QW-NiFe
ce:section.ce:para: Benzenediol isomers include hydroquinone (HQ), catechol (CC) and resorcinol (RC), which are common contaminants in water. These isomers exhibit different reducing properties due to the position of the phenolic hydroxyl groups, which can inhibit the catalytic activity of oxidase and oxidase-like analogues. Adding the same concentration of HQ, CC and RC to the 3-QW-NiFe-TMB system, the solutions showed different degrees of discolouration. From Fig. 3d, 3-QW-NiFe-TMB solution faded almost completely after adding HQ and the absorbance at 654 nm decreased the most, which can be concluded that HQ had the strongest reducing ability among the isomers. From above, the oxidase-like property of 3-QW-NiFe could be used for the colorimetric discrimination and quantitative detection of HQ, which was carried out using UV–vis titration experiments by varying the concentration of HQ. When HQ was introduced into the 3-QW-NiFe-TMB system, the electrons could be transferred to ox-TMB. Then, HQ was converted to p-benzoquinone and blue ox-TMB became colorless. From Fig. 3e, the absorbance of 3-QW-NiFe-TMB gradually decreased with the increase of HQ concentration. Fig. 3f showed that △A654 was proportional to the concentration of HQ in the range of 0.1 µM − 10 µM. The linear correction equation was △A654 = 0.0278 CHQ + 0.0272 (R2 = 0.990) and the LOD was calculated to be 0.014 μM, which was superior to most reported colorimetric assays (shown in Table 1 ). In addition, the 3-QW-NiFe-TMB system had good selectivity and anti-interference ability for HQ detection (Fig. S19). Therefore, the colorimetric method based on 3-QW-NiFe-TMB can be successfully used for the detection of HQ.

In order to develop an on-site visual detection method, a 3-QW-NiFe/smartphone sensor was constructed. The m-AP with different concentrations was added to the ox-ABTS system and the color changed from blue to pink, which was located in the visible light region. AcolorrecognizerAPPcalledColorPicker running on the smartphone was further used to convert the colorimetric results to the corresponding standard color cards and RGB values. As shown in Fig. 4 , an excellent linear relationship (Y = 0.065X + 0.816) with a regression coefficient (R2) of 0.993 between R/G and m-AP concentrations in the range of 0.5–12 μM was obtained,andtheLODwas0.08 μM. Besides, HQ could also be detected visually using this smartphone-assisted detection method (Fig. S20). Therefore, the proposed on-site visual detection method presented will be a promising strategy without need of analytical instruments, whichhasgreatapplicationpotentialinenvironmentalmonitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of m-Ap and HQ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the practical performance of the developed colorimetric method, the spiked m-AP and HQ were determined in real water samples, respectively. The real samples were pretreated as described in Section 1.7 in Supporting Information. The standard addition method showed that the recoveries of m-AP in lake water and tap water were 99.34 %-103.92 % and 99.51 %-101.05 %, respectively and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 4.84 % (Table S3). The recoveries and the RSD of HQ were also shown in Table S4. For comparison, the high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method was used to further determine m-Ap and HQ in the real samples. As shown in Tables S3 and S4, the m-Ap and HQ concentrations determined by the proposed sensor agreed with those given by HPLC method. These results confirmed that the proposed sensor based on 3-QW-NiFe hydrogel could be used for the quantitative determination of m-AP and HQ in real water samples.

In conclusion, a bimetallic hydrogel 3-QW-NiFe was designed and prepared by a simple and green synthesis method. The 3-QW-NiFe metallohydrogel exhibited peroxidase-like and oxidase-like activities. The catalytic mechanism was investigated by various spectra including UV–vis, fluorescence, and EPR spectra, which suggested that the peroxidase-like activity and the oxidase-like activity of 3-QW-NiFe metallohydrogel were realized by the hydroxyl radical and superoxide anion produced in the reaction process respectively. A colorimetric sensor based on the peroxidase-like activity of 3-QW-NiFe was constructed for m-AP with good selectivity and low LOD of 0.033 μM in a linear range from 0.5 to 12 μM. Then the colorimetric discrimination method was also constructed to detect HQ using the oxidase-like activity of 3-QW-NiFe. At the same time, the smartphone-assisted detection method will help to realize rapid and on-site visual detection without the need of any analytical instruments. In addition, the 3-QW-NiFe metallohydrogel-based assay can be successfully applied to detect phenolic pollutants in the water samples. With these advantages and the convenient operation, the proposed colorimetric assay for the detection of m-AP and HQ in this study can be applied for real-time environmental monitoring.

Xiao-Juan Wang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Yan Long: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Chuan-Wan Wei: Software, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Shu-Qin Gao: Project administration, Investigation, Data curation. Ying-Wu Lin: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21807058), and the Research Foundation of Education Bureau of Hunan Province (21A0279).
